subsystem: GPAC AVC/H.264 Sequence Parameter Set (SPS) bitstream parser. gf_avc_read_sps_bs_internal() decodes a raw SPS NAL unit (and, for subset SPS, the SVC/MVC extension) out of a GF_BitStream into an AVC_SPS record inside an AVCState. It is the code that turns an untrusted, network/file-delivered H.264 parameter set into the width/height/chroma/crop/VUI metadata the rest of the demuxer and decoder rely on.
provenance: Identified by the API surface and naming conventions: the gf_bs_* bitstream accessors, the GF_LOG/GF_LOG_CODING logging macros, GF_ARRAY_LENGTH, the GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT subset-SPS constant, the AVCState/AVC_SPS/GF_BitStream types and the AVC_SPS_PARSED/AVC_SUBSPS_PARSED state flags are all GPAC (libgpac, media_tools/av_parsers.c) idioms. The sample banner names it primevul_c_train_2034_vuln, and the function name gf_avc_read_sps_bs_internal is the exact GPAC internal SPS reader. The parsed syntax elements (profile_idc, sps_id, chroma_format_idc, poc_type, frame cropping, vui/hrd, svc extension) map 1:1 to ISO/IEC 14496-10 (H.264) seq_parameter_set_data.
trust_boundary: The GF_BitStream *bs is the trust boundary: every gf_bs_read_int_log / gf_bs_read_ue_log / gf_bs_read_se* call pulls attacker-controlled bits from a demuxed H.264 SPS NAL. An attacker who supplies an .mp4/.h264/RTP stream fully controls profile_idc, sps_id, chroma_format_idc, poc_cycle_length, pic_width/height_in_mbs, all four crop offsets, the VUI/HRD flags, and the SVC vui_ext_num_entries count. The exp-Golomb readers (gf_bs_read_ue_log) can return values up to ~2^32-1, so any field decoded that way is hostile and unbounded unless explicitly range-checked. bs, avc, and vui_flag_pos are caller-supplied pointers and may legitimately be NULL (the code itself checks bs for NULL).

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point. It takes a bitstream bs positioned at the start of an SPS, an AVCState avc that owns an array of AVC_SPS records, subseq_sps (0 for a base SPS, non-zero for a subset/SVC SPS), an out-parameter vui_flag_pos used by writers to record where the VUI flag sits, and nal_hdr signalling whether the NAL header was already consumed. sps is the AVC_SPS record that will be filled. sps_id is pre-initialised to -1 and is also the return value (a negative return means parse failure). mb_width/mb_height are signed macroblock dimensions; cl/cr/ct/cb are the four frame-crop offsets initialised to 0 so they can be stored even when cropping is absent. separate_colour_plane_flag defaults to 0.

When vui_flag_pos is NULL (the read path, as opposed to a rewrite path) the code enables emulation-prevention-byte removal on the bitstream so the 0x000003 escape sequences in the NAL are stripped as bits are consumed. Only AFTER that does it validate that bs itself is non-NULL and bail out with -1 if it is. The ordering matters: the first statement already passes bs into a bitstream helper.

If the caller did not already strip the 1-byte NAL header (nal_hdr==0) the parser consumes forbidden_zero_bit, nal_ref_idc and nal_unit_type. Then it reads the three fixed 8-bit fields profile_idc, profile_compatibility (pcomp) and level_idc. A cheap sanity gate rejects the SPS if the low two bits of pcomp are set (per the H.264 constraint that constraint_set flags reserved bits are zero). These reads are all fixed-width so their range is 0..255.

The seq_parameter_set_id is read as an unsigned exp-Golomb value, then GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT * subseq_sps is added so that subset SPS ids land in a separate id band and cannot overwrite base-layer SPS records. The sum is bounds-checked into [0,32): both a negative result (from a huge exp-Golomb value that overflows the s32) and an out-of-range positive result are rejected. sps is then aimed at avc->sps[sps_id], which must be a 32-entry array. chroma_format_idc and ChromaArrayType default to 1 (4:2:0). Critically, sps->state is OR'd with the PARSED flag here, at the very start, before the rest of the SPS has been decoded.

For High/SVC profiles the parser reads the chroma sub-structure. The switch deliberately falls through: profiles 100/110/122/244/44 first assert that the top three bits of pcomp are zero, then FALL INTO the 83/86/118/128 arm (no break after line 53) that reads chroma_format_idc (unsigned exp-Golomb) and stores it into ChromaArrayType. If chroma_format_idc==3 a separate_colour_plane_flag bit is read and, when set, forces ChromaArrayType back to 0. luma/chroma bit depths are read as exp-Golomb. If seq_scaling_matrix_present_flag is set, up to 8 scaling lists are walked with fully bounded fixed loops (k<8, z<sl where sl is 16 or 64), each running the standard delta-scale recurrence; no array is indexed by attacker data here.

The core SPS scalars are stored. log2_max_frame_num and (for poc_type 0) log2_max_poc_lsb are exp-Golomb + 4. For poc_type 1 the picture-order-count cycle is read: poc_cycle_length is an unsigned exp-Golomb count that then drives a write loop into the FIXED array sps->offset_for_ref_frame. This is the classic count-vs-fixed-buffer pattern, and here it is explicitly guarded: poc_cycle_length is rejected with -1 when it exceeds GF_ARRAY_LENGTH(sps->offset_for_ref_frame), so the loop writes at most GF_ARRAY_LENGTH elements at indices 0..N-1, staying in bounds.

poc_type is finally range-checked (>2 rejected). max_num_ref_frames is read but not bounded here. mb_width and mb_height are decoded from pic_width/height_in_mbs_minus1 exp-Golomb values plus one, then multiplied by 16 (and by (2 - frame_mbs_only_flag) for height) to yield pixel dimensions sps->width/height. These exp-Golomb inputs are attacker-controlled and unbounded, and there is no cap on mb_width/mb_height before the multiply.

If frame_cropping_flag is set, CropUnitX/Y are derived from the chroma format (SubWidthC/SubHeightC), and the four crop offsets cl/cr/ct/cb are read as unbounded exp-Golomb values. The width/height computed earlier are then reduced by CropUnitX*(cl+cr) and CropUnitY*(ct+cb). None of the crop offsets is validated against the frame dimensions, so the subtraction can drive width/height negative or wrap. The assert(SubWidthC == -1) documents the invariant that ChromaArrayType==0 implies no chroma subsampling was selected.

The (scaled) crop values are stored into sps->crop. If the caller passed a vui_flag_pos out-parameter, the current bit offset is recorded there (this write is correctly guarded by the non-NULL check). Then vui_parameters_present_flag is read to decide whether a VUI block follows.

The VUI block starts. aspect_ratio_idc is an 8-bit value; the Extended_SAR case (255) reads explicit 16-bit numerator/denominator, otherwise the index is bounds-checked (aspect_ratio_idc < GF_ARRAY_LENGTH(avc_hevc_sar)) before indexing the avc_hevc_sar table, and anything larger just logs a warning. This is a correctly guarded table lookup. overscan flags are read next.

Colour/signal-type defaults are set then optionally overwritten with read values, chroma location is optionally read, timing info reads two 32-bit ticks, and the NAL/VCL HRD parameter blocks are delegated to avc_parse_hrd_parameters(bs, &sps->vui.hrd) (not shown here). All fields are stored into the fixed sps->vui struct; no array indexed by attacker data is present in this block. The HRD sub-parser is a separate trust-sensitive routine whose bounds cannot be assessed from this file.

For subset SPS on SVC profiles (83/86) the seq_parameter_set_svc_extension is parsed. Notable is the SVC VUI extension loop: vui_ext_num_entries_minus1 is an unsigned exp-Golomb count and the loop condition i <= vui_ext_num_entries_minus1 iterates once per entry. Each iteration only consumes fixed-width bits (no array writes), so there is no memory-corruption surface, but the loop count itself is attacker-controlled and huge. MVC profiles (118/128) are explicitly not implemented and return early.

A trailing additional_extension2 flag, if set, causes an early return. Finally the function returns sps_id, the index of the SPS record that was populated (0..31), which callers use as a handle. On every failure path above the function instead returns a negative value.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Use-before-null-check. gf_bs_enable_emulation_byte_removal(bs, GF_TRUE) is invoked at line 10 before bs is validated for NULL at lines 13-15. The helper is a bitstream mutator and dereferences bs internally. The presence of the explicit `if (!bs) return -1;` guard proves the authors consider a NULL bs reachable, yet on the vui_flag_pos==NULL path (the normal read path) a NULL bs is dereferenced first. An attacker cannot set bs directly, but any caller that legitimately passes bs==NULL (the guard exists precisely for that) crashes with a NULL dereference before the guard runs. The invariant the implementation must uphold: validate bs != NULL BEFORE any use, i.e. hoist the null check above the emulation-removal call.

Unbounded macroblock dimensions and signed integer overflow. pic_width_in_mbs_minus1 and pic_height_in_map_units_minus1 are attacker-controlled unsigned exp-Golomb values with no upper bound before mb_width/mb_height (declared s32) are formed and multiplied by 16 (and by up to 2 for height). A crafted SPS can make mb_width*16 overflow s32, producing a wildly wrong or negative sps->width/height. These dimensions are later trusted by the demuxer/decoder to size frame buffers; a bogus dimension propagates into downstream allocation and stride math and is a latent driver of out-of-bounds writes or over/under-allocation in the consumer. The safe invariant is to bound the mb dimensions against the level's MaxFS/MaxWidth (or a sane absolute cap) before multiplying, and to compute in a width that cannot overflow.

Unvalidated crop offsets underflow the frame dimensions. The four crop offsets are unbounded exp-Golomb values, and CropUnitX*(cl+cr) / CropUnitY*(ct+cb) are subtracted from sps->width/height with no check that the crop is smaller than the picture. An attacker can set cl/cr/ct/cb so the subtraction underflows width/height (to a negative or, if the field is unsigned, a near-2^32 value). The H.264 spec requires the visible region after cropping to be positive; nothing here enforces that. Combined with the previous step, the resulting corrupt width/height is a latent memory-safety hazard wherever a caller allocates or iterates over width*height*bpp. The implementation must reject crops whose total exceeds the coded dimension (CropUnitX*(cl+cr) < width and CropUnitY*(ct+cb) < height) before subtracting.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
u32 vui_ext_num_entries_minus1 = gf_bs_read_ue_log(bs, "vui_ext_num_entries_minus1");

				for (i = 0; i <= vui_ext_num_entries_minus1; i++) {
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Attacker-controlled loop trip count (uncontrolled resource consumption / potential infinite loop). vui_ext_num_entries_minus1 is an unsigned exp-Golomb value up to ~2^32-1, and the loop runs i from 0 to that value inclusive. Because the counter i and the bound are both u32, a value of 0xFFFFFFFF makes `i <= vui_ext_num_entries_minus1` effectively never terminate on overflow (i wraps to 0 after 0xFFFFFFFF), and even short of that, billions of iterations each consume bits and logging. When the bitstream runs dry the gf_bs reads typically return 0 rather than aborting the loop, so this is a cheap denial-of-service: a tiny SPS forces an enormous parse loop. The implementation should cap vui_ext_num_entries_minus1 to a sane maximum and/or break when the bitstream is exhausted.

SPS marked PARSED before it is fully/validly parsed. sps->state gets the AVC_SPS_PARSED/AVC_SUBSPS_PARSED flag at the very top, yet the function can still return -1 at many later points (poc_cycle_length overflow line 111, poc_type>2 line 117, and every guard in the high-profile branch). After such a failure the SPS record has the PARSED bit set while its fields are only partially populated (or reflect a previous, different SPS at the same id). A consumer that keys off state to decide the record is usable will then read stale/partially-initialised width/height/chroma/crop values. This is an improper-initialisation / stale-state hazard: the invariant should be that the PARSED flag is set only on the success path, after all fields are written, so a failed parse never leaves a record that looks valid.

This is the count-vs-fixed-buffer pattern the method flags, and it must be confirmed rather than assumed. poc_cycle_length is an attacker-controlled exp-Golomb count that indexes the fixed array sps->offset_for_ref_frame. As written the guard `> GF_ARRAY_LENGTH(...)` is correct: it rejects counts strictly greater than the element count, so the loop writes at most GF_ARRAY_LENGTH elements at indices 0..N-1 and stays in bounds. The residual risk to verify is that GF_ARRAY_LENGTH truly resolves to the element count of offset_for_ref_frame (not sizeof in bytes) and that offset_for_ref_frame is a real fixed C array (not a pointer), because if GF_ARRAY_LENGTH is ever applied to a pointer or a byte size the guard becomes wrong and this converts to a CWE-787 out-of-bounds write.

summary: GPAC's untrusted-input H.264 SPS parser. The two headline count-vs-buffer surfaces (poc_cycle_length -> offset_for_ref_frame, aspect_ratio_idc -> avc_hevc_sar, sps_id -> avc->sps[]) are all explicitly bounds-checked, so the code is materially hardened against direct out-of-bounds writes. The remaining, real risks are: a NULL-dereference ordering bug (bs used before its NULL check), unbounded exp-Golomb dimensions/crop offsets that overflow or underflow sps->width/height and are trusted by downstream allocators (latent memory-safety), an attacker-controlled SVC vui_ext loop count (DoS), and premature setting of the PARSED state flag that can leave a partially-parsed record looking valid on error paths.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: gf_avc_read_sps_bs_internal(GF_BitStream *bs, AVCState *avc, u32 subseq_sps, u32 *vui_flag_pos, u32 nal_hdr) signature (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point / trust boundary; all pointer params are attacker-adjacent but the individual hazards are captured in the field-level anchors below.
bs dereferenced here before the NULL check at line 13 -> NULL-deref ordering bug.
The guard itself is correct; its problem is placement, reported under the line-10 finding.
Fixed-width 0..255 reads; pcomp&0x3 sanity gate present; no buffer or arithmetic hazard.
Unbounded exp-Golomb + shift, but immediately range-checked to [0,32) at line 35 including the negative/overflow case, so avc->sps[] index is safe.
Correct two-sided bound guarding the sps array index.
Index bounded to [0,32) by the preceding check; assumes avc->sps has >=32 entries, which is the caller contract.
PARSED flag set before parse completes; error returns leave a record flagged valid but partially populated.
Intentional fall-through after the pcomp&0xE0 gate; standard High/SVC chroma parse, no attacker-indexed access.
Unbounded but only used as an equality selector for SubWidthC/SubHeightC; out-of-range values leave SubWidthC=-1 and produce wrong (not unsafe) crop math.
Both loops are fixed-bound with no array indexed by attacker data; the delta recurrence is arithmetic on locals only.
Count-vs-fixed-buffer pattern (method requires a finding); guard is correct as written but must be confirmed that GF_ARRAY_LENGTH is element count and offset_for_ref_frame is a real array.
Range gate on poc_type; correct.
Stored scalar, not used to index anything in this function; downstream must bound it but no hazard here.
Unbounded exp-Golomb dimensions multiplied into s32; overflow yields corrupt dimensions trusted downstream.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: frame crop offsets cl/cr/ct/cb and width/height subtraction (lines 155-165)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Unvalidated crop offsets underflow sps->width/height; no positivity check after cropping.

In the ChromaArrayType==0 path SubWidthC provably stays -1 for all reachable chroma_format_idc/separate_colour_plane_flag combinations, so the assert holds; would at worst abort in debug builds (reachable-assert), not a memory hazard.
Write to caller out-param is correctly guarded by if (vui_flag_pos).
8-bit index; Extended_SAR (255) handled separately and the table index is bounds-checked before use, so avc_hevc_sar[] read is safe.
Delegated sub-parser not shown; its bounds cannot be assessed from this file (latent, out of scope of this anchor set).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: VUI timing/signal-type/colour reads into fixed sps->vui struct (lines 178-238)
reviewed_anchors.reason: All writes are into fixed-size struct scalars; no attacker-indexed array.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: vui_ext_num_entries_minus1 and for (i=0; i<=...; i++) loop (lines 271-296)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Attacker-controlled u32 trip count; unbounded iterations / wrap -> DoS.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: SVC extension fixed-width reads and se offsets (lines 247-268)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed-width bit reads and stored offsets only; no buffer indexed by attacker data.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: MVC (118/128) early return; additional_extension2 early return (lines 299-307)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Control-flow only; no memory or arithmetic hazard.
Only shifts subset-SPS ids into a separate band; result still bounds-checked at line 35.
Used correctly as an element-count bound in both call sites; its own correctness is a confirm item, not a distinct finding.

findings.title: Frame crop offsets underflow computed width/height (no positivity check)
findings.anchor: sps->width -= CropUnitX * (cl + cr); sps->height -= CropUnitY * (ct + cb);
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow of image dimensions from unvalidated crop (feeds downstream allocation)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0052_cwe415.c (dimensions consumed by implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: After cropping the visible dimensions must remain positive: CropUnitX*(cl+cr) < sps->width and CropUnitY*(ct+cb) < sps->height must be enforced before the subtraction, and crop offsets must be bounded before the CropUnit multiply.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker sets the four exp-Golomb crop offsets so that CropUnitX*(cl+cr) exceeds the coded width; the unsigned subtraction wraps sps->width to a near-2^32 value (or a signed negative). A demuxer/decoder that later allocates or strides over width*height*bpp then over- or under-allocates, a latent path to heap overflow or out-of-bounds access in the consumer.
findings.what_to_confirm: The type of sps->width/height (signed vs unsigned) and whether any caller re-validates the post-crop dimensions before using them for allocation/stride.

findings.title: Unbounded macroblock dimensions multiplied into width/height (signed overflow)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0052_cwe415.c (dimensions consumed by implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: pic_width_in_mbs_minus1 and pic_height_in_map_units_minus1 must be bounded (e.g. against the level's MaxFS/max frame width) before mb_width/mb_height are formed and multiplied by 16, and the multiply must be done in a width that cannot overflow.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted SPS supplies a huge exp-Golomb pic_width/height; mb_width/mb_height (s32) times 16 overflows to a wrong or negative sps->width/height. That corrupt dimension is trusted by the rest of GPAC for buffer sizing and iteration, producing over/under-allocation or bad stride math downstream.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether mb_width/mb_height are bounded anywhere before the *16, and how sps->width/height are used by allocators/decoders downstream.

findings.title: bs dereferenced before its NULL check
findings.invariant: bs must be validated non-NULL before any use; the NULL check must be hoisted above the gf_bs_enable_emulation_byte_removal call.
findings.failure_mode: When a caller passes bs==NULL together with vui_flag_pos==NULL (the read path), line 10 passes NULL into gf_bs_enable_emulation_byte_removal, which dereferences it, crashing before the guard at line 13 runs. The very existence of the guard shows NULL bs is an anticipated input, so this is a reachable NULL-deref DoS.
findings.what_to_confirm: That gf_bs_enable_emulation_byte_removal dereferences its bs argument (it configures the bitstream), and whether any caller invokes this function with a possibly-NULL bs on the vui_flag_pos==NULL path.

findings.title: Attacker-controlled SVC vui_ext loop trip count (DoS)
findings.code:
u32 vui_ext_num_entries_minus1 = gf_bs_read_ue_log(bs, "vui_ext_num_entries_minus1");

				for (i = 0; i <= vui_ext_num_entries_minus1; i++) {
findings.invariant: vui_ext_num_entries_minus1 must be capped to a sane maximum, and/or the loop must break when the bitstream is exhausted, so a small SPS cannot force an enormous parse.
findings.failure_mode: vui_ext_num_entries_minus1 is an unbounded exp-Golomb value up to ~2^32-1; the inclusive loop iterates that many times (and with 0xFFFFFFFF the u32 counter i wraps and the condition never becomes false). Each iteration consumes bits/logging; when the stream runs out the gf_bs reads return 0 rather than aborting, so a tiny crafted subset SPS causes a multi-second-to-unbounded CPU spin.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether gf_bs reads past end-of-buffer terminate the loop (return an error/EOS the loop honors) or simply return 0, and whether any upper bound is imposed on vui_ext_num_entries_minus1.

findings.title: SPS state flagged PARSED before parse completes / on error paths
findings.bug_class: Improper initialization / stale valid-state on failure
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0052_cwe415.c (state consumed by implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: The AVC_SPS_PARSED/AVC_SUBSPS_PARSED flag must be set only after a successful, complete parse, so that any function that returns -1 leaves the record un-flagged and consumers never treat a partial parse as valid.
findings.failure_mode: The PARSED bit is OR'd in at the top, but the function still returns -1 at lines 36, 53, 111, 117 and elsewhere. After such a failure sps->state advertises a valid, parsed SPS while width/height/chroma/crop are only partially written (or hold values from a previously parsed SPS at the same id). A consumer keying off state then reads stale/partially-initialised fields.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether callers gate use of avc->sps[id] on the PARSED flag, and whether that record can be observed after this function returns -1.

findings.title: poc_cycle_length bound into fixed offset_for_ref_frame (guarded; confirm GF_ARRAY_LENGTH semantics)
findings.invariant: GF_ARRAY_LENGTH(sps->offset_for_ref_frame) must equal the element count of a real fixed array, and the guard must reject poc_cycle_length > that count (which it does with strict >, since the loop writes indices 0..count-1).
findings.failure_mode: As written this is safe: poc_cycle_length (unbounded exp-Golomb) is rejected when greater than the array element count, so the write loop stays in bounds. It converts to a CWE-787 out-of-bounds write only if offset_for_ref_frame is ever changed to a pointer or GF_ARRAY_LENGTH is mis-defined to return a byte size, in which case the bound would be wrong.
findings.what_to_confirm: That sps->offset_for_ref_frame is a fixed C array (not a pointer) and that GF_ARRAY_LENGTH expands to sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]) (element count), not a byte count.

audit_checklist: Move the `if (!bs) return -1;` guard above the gf_bs_enable_emulation_byte_removal(bs, ...) call so bs is never dereferenced before validation.
Confirm GF_ARRAY_LENGTH(sps->offset_for_ref_frame) is element count and offset_for_ref_frame is a fixed array; the poc_cycle_length guard is only correct under that assumption.
Bound pic_width_in_mbs_minus1 / pic_height_in_map_units_minus1 before the *16 multiply and verify sps->width/height cannot overflow their type.
Enforce that CropUnitX*(cl+cr) < width and CropUnitY*(ct+cb) < height before subtracting the crop, so dimensions stay positive.
Cap vui_ext_num_entries_minus1 and/or break the SVC vui_ext loop on bitstream exhaustion to prevent a DoS spin.
Set AVC_SPS_PARSED/AVC_SUBSPS_PARSED only on the success path, and verify consumers do not act on avc->sps[id] after a -1 return.
Trace how downstream code consumes sps->width/height/max_num_ref_frames for allocation and stride, since corrupt values from here become memory-safety bugs there.
Audit avc_parse_hrd_parameters() (not shown) for its own bounds handling of cpb_cnt and per-entry bit reads.
